text: The surgeon divides the falciform ligament and the left triangular ligament, facilitating the retraction of the left lateral section and part of the left lobe of the liver towards the right side of the patient.

The surgeon divides the lesser omentum, specifically the pars flaccidae covering the chordate process and tumor. A Nathanson liver retractor is positioned beneath the left lateral section of the liver for enhanced exposure. The peritoneal reflections from the diaphragm are dissected as they join the lesser omentum.

The surgeon completes the opening of the pars flaccida and divides the lesser omentum up to the diaphragm level. The golf ball-sized tumor is visible inferiorly in the chordate process, while the superior caudate process appears relatively normal.

The surgeon dissects and divides the ligamentum venosum near its insertion into the leptopatic vein, elevating and retracting the tissue inferiorly to resect the entire fat pad including the ligament. Additional adhesions near the portal origin of the ligamentum venosum are removed, and the ligament is exposed and divided using an energy device.

The surgeon sets aside tissue to be included in the specimen bag for later retrieval. Fatty adhesions between the lesser omental fat and the chordate lobe are left attached to the tumor to avoid breaching the tumor capsule and ensure a clear margin. The hepatic artery is visible inferiorly, pulsing in the surgical field.

The surgeon carefully navigates around the rounded tumor, avoiding excessive pressure due to its soft nature. The inferior vena cava is visible, along with the first caudate tributary of the vena cava.

The surgeon identifies the bridge of the caudate process crossing behind the porta hepatis and in front of the inferior vena cava. Approaching the portal inflow to the caudate process, superficial tissue is divided to lengthen the area. A small branch is torn and grasped, then controlled using bipolar diathermy.
The surgeon uses an energy device to cauterize small vessels in the limited space, avoiding the use of clips to prevent congestion, in preparation for using a stapler later in the operation.

The surgeon carefully dissects and lengthens vessels using an energy device, avoiding damage to the inflow to the left lobe of the liver. Extra peritoneal attachments to the superior portion of the chordate process are dissected, gradually freeing the chordate process and tumor on three of four sides, leaving a bridge of chordate parenchyma to be stapled through.

The surgeon successfully lengthens the tissue between the porta hepatis and the caudate lobe, exposing the bridge of caudate parenchyma behind the porta hepatis. The bridge is divided on the patient's left side.

The surgeon identifies small tributaries of the inferior vena cava running through the bridge of chordate parenchyma. A prominent vessel is clipped to control the source of minor bleeding, while suction is used to clear the surgical field of persistent oozing.

The surgeon applies a locking Hem-o-lok clip on the side of the inferior vena cava to prevent metal clips from being dislodged by passing instruments, ensuring stability and safety during the procedure.

A gauze square is placed to control the ooze as the surgeon approaches the supralateral aspect of the inferior vena cava. The retrocaval ligament is being developed, revealing a vessel located very laterally at the start of the ligament. The chordate process is elevated to improve visibility, which is challenging due to its shape.

The surgeon uses a retracting instrument to elevate the superior aspect of the cordate lobe anteriorly, preparing a landing zone for the stapler. Ligamentous wrappings and short hepatic veins are gradually dealt with, including the identification, clipping, and division of a further short hepatic vein using an energy device.

The surgeon carefully dissects the areolar tissue in front of the inferior vena cava, avoiding avulsion of tributaries. The posterior superior corner of the caudate process is released, requiring persistence due to numerous attachments. The surgeon thins down the pre-caval bridge behind the porta hepatis, controlling small oozing vessels, and adjusts inflow to achieve adequate length for stapler passage.

The surgeon passes anteriorly to the left portal structures, thinning down the tissue to prevent the stapler from inadvertently including the tumor or unwanted structures, while managing minor oozing in the surgical field.

The surgical team actively uses a suction device to manage minor venous oozing while preparing the stapler. The surgeon lifts the caudate process forward, which stops the bleeding, allowing the stapler to be passed at the same angle as the suction device. Care is taken to ensure the stapler tips do not pinch the inferior vena cava or include the tumor.

The surgeon uses the stapler as a crushing device to thin down the parenchyma, advancing it slightly to utilize the full 60mm vascular load. After compressing for 30 to 60 seconds, the stapler is fired. A second stapler is introduced to address the remaining small bridge of tissue in front of the inferior vena cava, ensuring the tumor is kept out of the staple line.

The surgeon carefully positions the stapler near the diaphragm and pericardium, ensuring it is not forced further. The specimen is temporarily set aside while the surgical field is checked for any significant bleeding, which appears to be minimal.

The surgeon places the fat from around the chest ligament with the specimen, introducing a bag for specimen removal. The specimen is removed through an incisional hernia site, avoiding pelvic adhesions. After cleaning, fibrillar is applied to stop the remaining ooze.

The surgeon uses a sucker to explore the surgical site, identifying a vessel that has opened up, likely an arterial branch. Despite poor exposure, metallic clips are applied to the vessel to control bleeding, ensuring the clips remain secure without interference from a stapler.

The surgeon places a stapler on the tissue without needing to encircle it entirely, unlike locking clips. After addressing mesenteric ooze from adhesiolysis, additional fibrillar is applied. The abdomen is closed without a drain, and the patient is discharged home after five days.
